danger_signals.signal: Enemy troop presence in adjacent districts
danger_signals.visual_form: Unit icons/markers on district map showing enemy military concentration
danger_signals.meaning: Immediate threat to player-controlled territory; risk of invasion or battle

danger_signals.visual_form: Quarterly budget review interface with reduced numerical allocations
danger_signals.meaning: Political dissatisfaction; indicates weakening support and potential ouster as Commander-in-Chief

danger_signals.visual_form: District color/ownership marker changes on strategic map
danger_signals.meaning: Territorial loss to enemy; reduces resource base and strategic position

danger_signals.visual_form: Patriot/Loyalist militia units moving or attacking without player command
danger_signals.meaning: Unstable political support; civilian forces acting independently indicate morale or support issues

safety_signals.visual_form: Fort/fortress structures shown on hex-grid battlefields and district maps
safety_signals.meaning: Defensive strongholds provide protection against enemy advances; strategic anchors

safety_signals.visual_form: District morale/popularity indicators increase after parade action
safety_signals.meaning: Improved civilian support; maintains political approval and budget allocations

safety_signals.signal: Accumulated allied troops in adjacent districts
safety_signals.visual_form: Multiple unit markers in neighboring districts ready to reinforce
safety_signals.meaning: Defensive depth; ability to call reinforcements to threatened areas

safety_signals.visual_form: Naval unit positioning and barrages available on coastal hex-grids
safety_signals.meaning: Supply line security and additional defensive/offensive capability

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Patriot militia (civilian forces supporting American cause)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Districts controlled by American forces; headquarters tied to player's C-in-C selection

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Loyalist militia (civilian forces supporting British crown); German mercenary regiments
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Districts controlled by British forces; fortified positions in major population centers

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Independent militia units with American sympathies
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Run districts autonomously; attack/annex adjacent territories without direct command
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Scattered throughout Thirteen Colonies; can be recruited under American commander authority

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Independent militia and foreign mercenary units with British sympathies
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Operate autonomously; collect resources and expand territorial control without orders
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Organized and funded by British; concentrated in strategically valuable districts

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Limited American territorial control concentrated in New England; scattered districts with lower unit counts
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: American player must adopt defensive posture, focus on holding ground, slowly gather strength over multiple turns

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: British control major coastal cities and fortified positions; larger initial territory holdings
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: British player must fortify existing positions and quickly move to eliminate American forces before they gain strength

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Budget allocations show increased military spending; militia units remain under faction control; district morale indicators stable
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player can expand military operations; militia units support faction objectives; higher-ranked commanders attract additional units

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Budget allocations reduce; civilian political entities begin voting on C-in-C approval; militia units show independent movement
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Military spending becomes restricted; player must spend actions on parades and political management to maintain support; risk of ouster

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Hex-grid terrain shows seasonal variations affecting movement and tactical positioning
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Different unit types have varying effectiveness across terrain; cavalry limited in non-open terrain; guerrilla units gain mobility advantage

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Naval units positioned in coastal districts; barrages available as tactical support; transport capability visual indicator
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Coastal districts become easier to defend/attack; troops can be transported via naval routes; enemies have reduced coastal access

environmental_storytelling.technique: Historical accuracy in district layout and geography
environmental_storytelling.example: Thirteen Colonies mapped with historical fidelity; players experience actual Revolutionary War geography and strategic chokepoints
American side starts weak and must defend; British side starts strong and must attack quickly—narrative embedded in game mechanics
Actions available in each district (purchasing materials, bribing generals, holding parades) communicate civilian-military relationship and political pressures

environmental_storytelling.technique: Independent militia behavior as world-state indicator
environmental_storytelling.example: Patriot and Loyalist militia running districts and expanding territory autonomously reflects historical reality of decentralized colonial resistance and British loyalist movements
Choosing between George Washington, Artemas Ward, or Charles Lee (Americans) or Gage, Clinton, or Howe (British) connects player to actual historical leadership and their documented capabilities
Continental Congress/British Parliament budget meetings quarterly communicate cyclical political pressure and dependency on civilian support for military funding

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Will expand into adjacent districts, attack enemy positions, and collect resources without direct player command; indicates civilian mobilization
Organize and expand territory under British strategy; aggressive expansion indicates strong British civilian/foreign support
Equal or higher-rank commanders automatically attract militia units, swelling their forces—communicates hierarchical military structure
Vote to oust Commander-in-Chief; reduce budget allocations—signals withdrawal of political mandate
Can move through and exploit difficult terrain; use surprise attacks—tactical tell of unconventional warfare
Deploy with devastating shock charges; become immobilized in difficult terrain—terrain-dependent power fantasy
Provide barrages and troop transport; unavailable inland—communicates logistics and supply-line importance

notes: Liberty or Death is a historically-grounded turn-based strategy game where world-state manifestation is primarily mechanical and numerical rather than graphically dramatic. Political approval and budget allocation are the primary danger/safety signals, creating asymmetrical gameplay between American (defensive) and British (offensive) sides. The game communicates faction alignment through independent militia behavior—a key manifestation of how civilian political entities impact military strategy. Environmental storytelling is driven by historical accuracy, district management mechanics, and the quarterly political review system. The game lacks visually dramatic manifestations (reviewers noted it requires 'studying changing numbers rather than watching exciting graphics'), meaning manifestation is almost entirely conveyed through interface elements, numerical indicators, and mechanical behavior rather than cinematic visual tells.
